docs.title: D4-Diag
docs.text: D4-Diag is a Python code analysis and visualization tool that generates interactive Mermaid diagrams to help you understand your codebase structure.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• 🏗️ Architecture Overview - Visualize your project structure with files as containers showing classes and functions
  • 📊 Class Diagrams - See all classes with their methods and inheritance relationships
  • 🔗 Module Dependencies - Understand import relationships between your Python modules
  • 🎨 Interactive Viewer - Browse all diagrams in a beautiful web interface
  • ⚡ Fast Analysis - Quickly analyze entire projects or individual files

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
# Analyze a project
poetry run main /path/to/your/project

# View the generated diagrams
poetry run viewer /path/to/your/project/docs/diagrams

docs.title: Architecture Diagram
docs.text: Shows your project's file structure with each file as a subgraph containing its classes and functions. Import relationships are shown as arrows between files.

docs.title: Class Diagram
docs.text: A proper UML-style class diagram showing all classes, their methods, and inheritance relationships using Mermaid's classDiagram syntax.

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text: A clean dependency graph showing which modules import from which, helping you understand coupling and module organization.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
Get started with D4-Diag in seconds:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git
cd d4-diag

# Install with Poetry
poetry install

# Run analysis
poetry run main .

See the Installation Guide for more details.

docs.title: Why D4-Diag?
docs.text:
Traditional code analysis tools often produce overwhelming output or require complex setup. D4-Diag focuses on:

  • Simplicity - Single command to analyze and visualize
  • Clarity - Clean, focused diagrams that show what matters
  • Speed - Fast AST-based analysis
  • Interactivity - Beautiful web viewer with lazy rendering for large projects

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start Guide - Get up and running in 5 minutes
  • Analyzing Code - Learn all analysis options
  • Diagram Types - Understand each diagram type
  • Examples - See real-world usage examples

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for considering contributing to D4-Diag!

docs.title: Fork and Clone
docs.text:
# Fork on GitHub, then clone your fork
git clone https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git
cd d4-diag

# Add upstream remote
git remote add upstream https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
poetry install

# Run tests
poetry run pytest

# Analyze d4-diag itself
poetry run main .
poetry run viewer docs/diagrams

docs.title: 1. Create a Branch
docs.text: git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

docs.title: 2. Make Changes
docs.text: Follow these guidelines: - Write clear, descriptive commit messages - Add tests for new features - Update documentation - Follow existing code style

docs.title: 3. Test Your Changes
docs.text:
# Run tests
poetry run pytest

# Test on real projects
poetry run main /path/to/test/project
poetry run viewer /path/to/test/project/docs/diagrams

docs.title: 4. Submit Pull Request
docs.text:
# Push to your fork
git push origin feature/your-feature-name

# Create PR on GitHub

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8
  • Use type hints where helpful
  • Keep functions focused and small
  • Add docstrings for public APIs

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
def sanitize_id(name: str) -> str:
    """Sanitize a string for use as a Mermaid node ID.

    Args:
        name: Raw string to sanitize

    Returns:
        Sanitized ID safe for Mermaid
    """
    s = re.sub(r'[^\w]', '_', name)
    if s and s[0].isdigit():
        s = 'n' + s
    return 'id_' + s

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# All tests
poetry run pytest

# Specific test file
poetry run pytest tests/test_analyzer.py

# With coverage
poetry run pytest --cov=d4_diag

docs.title: Writing Tests
docs.text:
Add tests in tests/ directory:

def test_sanitize_id():
    assert sanitize_id("models.py") == "id_models_py"
    assert sanitize_id("123test") == "id_n123test"

docs.title: Building Docs Locally
docs.text:
# Install docs dependencies
poetry install

# Serve docs locally
poetry run mkdocs serve

# Build docs
poetry run mkdocs build

docs.title: Writing Docs
docs.text:
• Use clear, concise language
  • Include code examples
  • Add screenshots where helpful
  • Update navigation in mkdocs.yml

docs.title: High Priority
docs.text:
• [ ] Add support for more diagram types
  • [ ] Improve import resolution for complex projects
  • [ ] Add filtering options (exclude patterns, etc.)
  • [ ] Performance optimization for very large projects

docs.title: Medium Priority
docs.text:
• [ ] Add configuration file support
  • [ ] Export diagrams to PNG/SVG
  • [ ] Interactive diagram editing
  • [ ] Plugin system for custom analyzers

docs.title: Low Priority
docs.text:
• [ ] Support for other languages (JavaScript, TypeScript)
  • [ ] Integration with IDEs
  • [ ] Diagram diff tool
  • [ ] Web-based diagram editor

docs.title: Bug Reports
docs.text: Include: - Python version - D4-Diag version - Steps to reproduce - Expected vs actual behavior - Sample code if possible

docs.title: Feature Requests
docs.text: Include: - Use case description - Proposed solution - Alternative approaches considered - Willingness to implement

docs.title: Code Review Process
docs.text:
1. Maintainer reviews PR
  2. Feedback provided via comments
  3. Author addresses feedback
  4. Maintainer approves and merges

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Maintainers handle releases:

  1. Update version in pyproject.toml
  2. Update CHANGELOG.md
  3. Create GitHub release
  4. Publish to PyPI (future)

docs.title: Examples
docs.text: Real-world usage examples for D4-Diag.

docs.title: Example 1: Analyzing a Flask Application
docs.text:
# Clone a sample Flask app
git clone https://github.com/example/flask-app.git
cd flask-app

# Analyze the application
poetry run main app/

# View diagrams
poetry run viewer app/docs/diagrams

What you'll see: - Architecture showing routes, models, and utilities - Class diagram with SQLAlchemy models - Module dependencies showing app structure

docs.title: Example 2: Understanding a Django Project
docs.text:
# Navigate to Django project
cd my-django-project

# Analyze specific apps
poetry run main myapp/ accounts/ api/

# View diagrams
poetry run viewer docs/diagrams

Insights: - See which apps import from which - Understand model inheritance - Identify circular dependencies

docs.title: Example 3: Documenting a Library
docs.text:
# Analyze your library
cd my-library
poetry run main src/

# Commit diagrams to repo
git add docs/diagrams/*.mmd
git commit -m "Add architecture diagrams"

Benefits: - Auto-generated documentation - Visual API overview - Easier onboarding

docs.title: Example 4: Code Review
docs.text:
Before reviewing a pull request:

# Checkout PR branch
git checkout pr-123

# Analyze changed modules
poetry run main src/models/ src/services/

# Compare with main branch
git checkout main
poetry run main src/models/ src/services/

# View both sets of diagrams

Use cases: - Verify architectural changes - Spot unintended dependencies - Review class design

docs.title: Example 5: Refactoring Planning
docs.text:
# Analyze current state
poetry run main src/
cp -r docs/diagrams docs/diagrams-before

# After refactoring
poetry run main src/
cp -r docs/diagrams docs/diagrams-after

# Compare
diff docs/diagrams-before/ docs/diagrams-after/

Helps with: - Visualizing impact of changes - Ensuring dependencies are simplified - Documenting refactoring decisions

docs.title: Example 6: Microservices Architecture
docs.text:
# Analyze each service
for service in service-a service-b service-c; do
    poetry run main $service/
    mv $service/docs/diagrams docs/$service-diagrams
done

# View all services
poetry run viewer docs/service-a-diagrams
poetry run viewer docs/service-b-diagrams
poetry run viewer docs/service-c-diagrams

Insights: - Compare service complexity - Identify shared patterns - Plan service boundaries

docs.title: Example 7: Test Coverage Visualization
docs.text:
# Analyze source code
poetry run main src/
mv docs/diagrams docs/src-diagrams

# Analyze tests
poetry run main tests/
mv docs/diagrams docs/test-diagrams

# Compare to see what's tested

Reveals: - Which classes have test coverage - Test organization - Missing test files

docs.title: Example 8: Programmatic Usage
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate diagrams for multiple projects"""

from pathlib import Path
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

projects = [
    "/path/to/project-a",
    "/path/to/project-b",
    "/path/to/project-c"
]

for project_root in projects:
    print(f"\nAnalyzing {project_root}...")

    # Find files
    files = find_python_files(project_root)

    # Analyze
    analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root)
    analyzer.build_module_map(files)

    for f in files:
        analyzer.analyze_file(f)

    # Generate
    output = Path(project_root) / "docs" / "diagrams"
    analyzer.generate_all(str(output))

    print(f"  Generated diagrams in {output}")

docs.title: Example 9: CI/CD Integration
docs.text:
Add to .github/workflows/diagrams.yml:

name: Generate Diagrams

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  diagrams:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Setup Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.10'

      - name: Install Poetry
        run: curl -sSL https://install.python-poetry.org | python3 -

      - name: Install D4-Diag
        run: |
          git clone https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git
          cd d4-diag
          poetry install

      - name: Generate Diagrams
        run: |
          cd d4-diag
          poetry run main ../src

      - name: Commit Diagrams
        run: |
          git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
          git config user.email "actions@github.com"
          git add docs/diagrams/*.mmd
          git diff --quiet && git diff --staged --quiet || git commit -m "Update diagrams"
          git push

docs.title: Example 10: Embedding in README
docs.text:
After generating diagrams, embed them in your README:

# My Project

## Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph models.py
        User["User (3 methods)"]
        Post["Post (5 methods)"]
    end

    subgraph views.py
        index["index"]
        profile["profile"]
    end

    views.py --> models.py
```

See [full diagrams](docs/diagrams/) for details.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• User Guide - Detailed usage
  • CLI Reference - All commands
  • Contributing - Add your own examples

docs.title: Code Quality
docs.text: D4-Diag uses several tools to maintain code quality and consistency.

docs.title: Black - Code Formatter
docs.text:
Black is an opinionated Python code formatter that ensures consistent code style.

Configuration: pyproject.toml 

[tool.black]
line-length = 100
target-version = ['py38', 'py39', 'py310', 'py311']

Usage: 

# Format all files
poetry run black .

# Check without modifying
poetry run black --check .

# Format specific files
poetry run black src/d4_diag/main.py

docs.title: Flake8 - Linter
docs.text:
Flake8 is a linting tool that checks for code style issues, potential bugs, and complexity.

Configuration: .flake8 

[flake8]
max-line-length = 100
extend-ignore = E203, E266, E501, W503, F541
max-complexity = 20

Usage: 

# Check all source files
poetry run flake8 src/

# Check specific files
poetry run flake8 src/d4_diag/main.py

# Check with specific rules
poetry run flake8 --select=E,W src/

Common Error Codes: - E: PEP 8 errors - W: PEP 8 warnings - F: PyFlakes errors (undefined names, unused imports) - C: McCabe complexity - N: PEP 8 naming conventions

docs.title: Pre-commit - Git Hooks
docs.text:
Pre-commit runs checks automatically before each commit.

Configuration: .pre-commit-config.yaml

Setup: 

# Install hooks
poetry run pre-commit install

# Run manually on all files
poetry run pre-commit run --all-files

# Run on specific files
poetry run pre-commit run --files src/d4_diag/main.py

Hooks configured: 1. trailing-whitespace - Remove trailing whitespace 2. end-of-file-fixer - Ensure files end with newline 3. check-yaml - Validate YAML files 4. check-json - Validate JSON files 5. check-toml - Validate TOML files 6. black - Auto-format Python code 7. flake8 - Lint Python code 8. isort - Sort imports

docs.title: Before Committing
docs.text:
# 1. Format code with black
poetry run black .

# 2. Check with flake8
poetry run flake8 src/

# 3. Run all pre-commit hooks
poetry run pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: During Development
docs.text:
# Format on save (configure your IDE)
# Or run black periodically
poetry run black src/d4_diag/

# Check specific file
poetry run flake8 src/d4_diag/main.py

docs.title: CI/CD Integration
docs.text:
Add to your CI pipeline:

# .github/workflows/quality.yml
name: Code Quality

on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.8'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install poetry
          poetry install

      - name: Run black
        run: poetry run black --check .

      - name: Run flake8
        run: poetry run flake8 src/

      - name: Run pre-commit
        run: poetry run pre-commit run --all-files

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text:
Install extensions: - Black Formatter - Format on save - Flake8 - Real-time linting

Settings (.vscode/settings.json): 

{
  "[python]": {
    "editor.formatOnSave": true,
    "editor.defaultFormatter": "ms-python.black-formatter"
  },
  "python.linting.enabled": true,
  "python.linting.flake8Enabled": true,
  "python.linting.flake8Args": ["--config=.flake8"]
}

docs.title: PyCharm
docs.text:
1. Black:
  2. Settings → Tools → External Tools → Add Black

  3. Configure as file watcher for auto-format

  4. Flake8:

  5. Settings → Tools → External Tools → Add Flake8
  6. Or use built-in inspections

docs.title: Black
docs.text:
Black is opinionated and doesn't support ignoring specific lines. To exclude files:

[tool.black]
exclude = '''
/(
    \.git
  | \.venv
  | migrations
)/
'''

docs.title: Flake8
docs.text:
Inline ignore: 

# Ignore specific error on this line
result = some_long_function_call()  # noqa: E501

# Ignore multiple errors
import unused_module  # noqa: F401,E402

File-level ignore: 

# flake8: noqa
# At top of file to ignore all errors

Config-level ignore: 

# .flake8
per-file-ignores =
    __init__.py:F401
    tests/*:E501

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Run black before committing - Ensures consistent formatting
  2. Fix flake8 issues - Address linting errors before pushing
  3. Use pre-commit hooks - Automate quality checks
  4. Configure IDE - Get real-time feedback
  5. Don't ignore warnings - Fix issues rather than suppressing them
  6. Keep complexity low - Refactor complex functions (C901 warnings)

docs.title: Black and Flake8 Conflicts
docs.text: Some flake8 rules conflict with black's formatting. These are already ignored in .flake8: - E203 - Whitespace before ':' - E501 - Line too long (black handles this) - W503 - Line break before binary operator

docs.title: Pre-commit Fails
docs.text:
# Update hooks
poetry run pre-commit autoupdate

# Clear cache
poetry run pre-commit clean

# Reinstall
poetry run pre-commit uninstall
poetry run pre-commit install

docs.title: Flake8 Too Strict
docs.text:
Adjust .flake8 configuration: 

# Increase complexity threshold
max-complexity = 20

# Ignore additional rules
extend-ignore = E203, E266, E501, W503, F541

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Black Documentation
  • Flake8 Documentation
  • Pre-commit Documentation
  • PEP 8 Style Guide

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.8 or higher
  • Poetry (recommended) or pip

docs.title: Using Poetry (Recommended)
docs.text: Poetry manages dependencies and virtual environments automatically.

docs.title: Install Poetry
docs.text:
If you don't have Poetry installed:

# On Windows (PowerShell)
(Invoke-WebRequest -Uri https://install.python-poetry.org -UseBasicParsing).Content | python -

# On macOS/Linux
curl -sSL https://install.python-poetry.org | python3 -

docs.title: Install D4-Diag
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git
cd d4-diag

# Install dependencies
poetry install

# Verify installation
poetry run main --help

docs.title: Using pip
docs.text:
If you prefer pip:

# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/internetics-net/d4-diag.git
cd d4-diag

# Create virtual environment
python -m venv venv

# Activate virtual environment
# On Windows
venv\Scripts\activate
# On macOS/Linux
source venv/bin/activate

# Install in development mode
pip install -e .

# Verify installation
python -m d4_diag.main --help

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Test that everything works:

# Analyze the d4-diag project itself
poetry run main .

# View the generated diagrams
poetry run viewer docs/diagrams

Your browser should open showing three interactive diagrams.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start Guide - Learn basic usage
  • Analyzing Code - Detailed analysis options

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get up and running with D4-Diag in 5 minutes.

docs.title: 1. Analyze a Project
docs.text:
Point D4-Diag at any Python project or file:

# Analyze entire project
poetry run main /path/to/project

# Analyze specific directory
poetry run main /path/to/project/src

# Analyze single file
poetry run main script.py

docs.title: 2. View the Diagrams
docs.text:
D4-Diag generates diagrams in <project>/docs/diagrams/:

poetry run viewer /path/to/project/docs/diagrams

Your browser opens showing three tabs: - Architecture - File structure with classes/functions - Class Diagram - UML-style class relationships - Module Dependencies - Import graph

docs.title: Example Walkthrough
docs.text:
Let's analyze a sample project:

# Create a simple Python project
mkdir my-project
cd my-project

# Create some Python files
cat > models.py << 'EOF'
class User:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

    def greet(self):
        return f"Hello, {self.name}"

class Admin(User):
    def __init__(self, name, level):
        super().__init__(name)
        self.level = level
EOF

cat > main.py << 'EOF'
from models import User, Admin

def create_user(name):
    return User(name)

def main():
    user = create_user("Alice")
    print(user.greet())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
EOF

# Analyze it
poetry run main .

# View diagrams
poetry run viewer docs/diagrams

docs.title: Class Diagram
docs.text:
User
    ├─ __init__()
    └─ greet()
        ▲
        │
      Admin
      └─ __init__()

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text: main.py ──> models.py

docs.title: Analyze Multiple Paths
docs.text: poetry run main src/ tests/ scripts/

docs.title: Analyze Current Directory
docs.text:
cd /path/to/project
poetry run main .

docs.title: Custom Output Location
docs.text: Diagrams are always saved to <project_root>/docs/diagrams/. The project root is determined by: 1. First directory argument (if analyzing a directory) 2. Parent directory of first file (if analyzing files) 3. Current working directory (as fallback)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Analyzing Code - Advanced analysis options
  • Diagram Types - Understand each diagram
  • Viewing Diagrams - Viewer features

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Use D4-Diag programmatically in your Python code.

docs.title: CodeMapAnalyzer
docs.text: Main class for code analysis and diagram generation.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer

analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root: str)

Parameters: - project_root (str): Absolute path to project root directory

Attributes: - files (Dict[str, dict]): Per-file analysis data - import_edges (Set[tuple]): Import relationships between files - project_root (str): Project root path

docs.title: build_module_map
docs.text:
Build mapping from module names to file paths.

analyzer.build_module_map(file_paths: List[str])

Parameters: - file_paths (List[str]): List of absolute file paths to analyze

Returns: None

Side effects: Populates internal _module_map for import resolution

docs.title: analyze_file
docs.text:
Analyze a single Python file.

analyzer.analyze_file(file_path: str)

Parameters: - file_path (str): Absolute path to Python file

Returns: None

Side effects: Updates files dict with analysis results

Raises: - Prints syntax error message if file has invalid Python syntax

docs.title: generate_architecture
docs.text:
Generate architecture overview diagram.

analyzer.generate_architecture(output_file: str)

Parameters: - output_file (str): Path where diagram will be saved

Returns: None

Side effects: Writes Mermaid diagram to file

docs.title: generate_class_diagram
docs.text:
Generate UML class diagram.

analyzer.generate_class_diagram(output_file: str)

Parameters: - output_file (str): Path where diagram will be saved

Returns: None

Side effects: Writes Mermaid diagram to file

docs.title: generate_module_deps
docs.text:
Generate module dependency diagram.

analyzer.generate_module_deps(output_file: str)

Parameters: - output_file (str): Path where diagram will be saved

Returns: None

Side effects: Writes Mermaid diagram to file

docs.title: generate_all
docs.text:
Generate all three diagram types.

analyzer.generate_all(output_dir: str)

Parameters: - output_dir (str): Directory where diagrams will be saved

Returns: None

Side effects: - Creates output directory if it doesn't exist - Writes three .mmd files to output directory

docs.title: print_summary
docs.text:
Print analysis summary to stdout.

analyzer.print_summary()

Returns: None

Side effects: Prints formatted summary to stdout

docs.title: sanitize_id
docs.text:
Sanitize a string for use as a Mermaid node ID.

from d4_diag.main import sanitize_id

node_id = sanitize_id(name: str) -> str

Parameters: - name (str): Raw string to sanitize

Returns: str - Sanitized ID safe for Mermaid

Example: 

sanitize_id("models.py")  # Returns: "id_models_py"
sanitize_id("User")       # Returns: "id_User"

docs.title: qlabel
docs.text:
Quote a label for safe Mermaid rendering.

from d4_diag.main import qlabel

label = qlabel(text: str) -> str

Parameters: - text (str): Text to quote

Returns: str - Quoted text safe for Mermaid labels

Example: 

qlabel("User (3 methods)")  # Returns: '"User (3 methods)"'

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
import os
from pathlib import Path
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

# Setup
project_root = "/path/to/project"
output_dir = os.path.join(project_root, "docs", "diagrams")

# Find all Python files
file_paths = find_python_files(project_root)
print(f"Found {len(file_paths)} Python files")

# Create analyzer
analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root)

# Build module map for import resolution
analyzer.build_module_map(file_paths)

# Analyze all files
for file_path in file_paths:
    analyzer.analyze_file(file_path)

# Print summary
analyzer.print_summary()

# Generate diagrams
analyzer.generate_all(output_dir)

print(f"Diagrams saved to {output_dir}")

docs.title: File Info
docs.text:
Each entry in analyzer.files has this structure:

{
    'classes': [
        {
            'name': str,           # Class name
            'methods': [str],      # List of method names
            'bases': [str]         # List of base class names
        }
    ],
    'functions': [str],            # List of function names
    'imports': [str]               # List of imported modules
}

docs.title: Import Edge
docs.text:
Each entry in analyzer.import_edges is a tuple:

(source_rel: str, target_rel: str)

Where paths are relative to project_root.

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text: Complete command-line interface reference for D4-Diag.

docs.title: Main Command
docs.text: Analyze Python code and generate diagrams.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: poetry run main <path> [path2 ...]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Type Required Description <path> string Yes Python file or directory to analyze [path2 ...] string No Additional paths to analyze

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Single file
poetry run main app.py

# Single directory
poetry run main src/

# Multiple paths
poetry run main src/ tests/ scripts/

# Current directory
poetry run main .

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Generates three Mermaid diagram files in <project_root>/docs/diagrams/: - architecture.mmd - Architecture overview - class_diagram.mmd - Class diagram - module_deps.mmd - Module dependencies

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Success 1 No arguments provided 1 No Python files found

docs.title: Viewer Command
docs.text: Launch interactive diagram viewer.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: poetry run viewer <diagrams_directory>

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text: Argument Type Required Description <diagrams_directory> string Yes Directory containing .mmd files

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# View diagrams from analyzed project
poetry run viewer /path/to/project/docs/diagrams

# View diagrams from current directory
poetry run viewer docs/diagrams

docs.title: Behavior
docs.text:
1. Scans directory for .mmd files
  2. Generates temporary HTML file
  3. Opens HTML in default browser
  4. Displays interactive diagram viewer

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
Scanning for diagrams in: /path/to/diagrams

Found 3 diagram file(s):
  - architecture.mmd
  - class_diagram.mmd
  - module_deps.mmd

Generated viewer: /tmp/tmpXXXXXX.html
Opening in browser...

docs.title: Exit Codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 0 Success 1 No arguments provided 1 Directory not found 1 No .mmd files found

docs.title: Python Version
docs.text: Requires Python 3.8 or higher.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text:
Runtime dependencies: - Python standard library only

Development dependencies: - pytest (testing) - mkdocs (documentation) - mkdocs-material (documentation theme)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: D4-Diag has no configuration files. All behavior is controlled via command-line arguments.

docs.title: Programmatic Usage
docs.text:
You can also use D4-Diag as a Python module:

from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer

# Create analyzer
analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root="/path/to/project")

# Build module map
analyzer.build_module_map(file_paths)

# Analyze files
for file_path in file_paths:
    analyzer.analyze_file(file_path)

# Generate diagrams
analyzer.generate_all(output_dir="docs/diagrams")

See API Reference for details.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• API Reference - Programmatic usage
  • Examples - Real-world usage examples

docs.title: Analyzing Code
docs.text: Learn how to use D4-Diag's analysis capabilities effectively.

docs.title: Basic Syntax
docs.text: poetry run main <path> [path2 ...]

docs.title: Arguments
docs.text:
• <path> - Python file or directory to analyze
  • Multiple paths can be provided

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
# Single file
poetry run main app.py

# Single directory
poetry run main src/

# Multiple paths
poetry run main src/ tests/ scripts/

# Current directory
poetry run main .

docs.title: What Gets Analyzed
docs.text: D4-Diag performs static analysis using Python's AST (Abstract Syntax Tree):

docs.title: Detected Elements
docs.text:
• Classes - All class definitions with their methods
  • Functions - Top-level function definitions
  • Imports - Import statements (import and from ... import)
  • Inheritance - Base classes for each class

docs.title: Not Analyzed
docs.text:
• Runtime behavior
  • Dynamic imports
  • Decorators (shown but not analyzed)
  • Type hints (shown but not analyzed)

docs.title: Output Location
docs.text:
Diagrams are generated in: 

<project_root>/docs/diagrams/
├── architecture.mmd
├── class_diagram.mmd
└── module_deps.mmd

The project root is determined by: 1. First directory in arguments → use as root 2. Only files provided → use parent directory of first file 3. Fallback → current working directory

docs.title: Analysis Summary
docs.text:
After analysis, you'll see a summary:

=== Code Map Summary ===
Files analyzed:   32
Classes found:    59
Functions found:  56
Import links:     37

Project structure:
  src/models.py  (16 classes)
  src/server.py  (3 classes)
  src/main.py  (5 functions)
  ...

docs.title: Large Projects
docs.text:
D4-Diag is optimized for large codebases:

  • Fast AST parsing - No code execution required
  • Deduplication - Import edges are deduplicated
  • Lazy rendering - Viewer only renders visible diagrams
  • Scrollable output - Large diagrams are scrollable

docs.title: Performance Tips
docs.text:
For very large projects (100+ files):

  1. Analyze specific subdirectories instead of entire repo
  2. Exclude test files if not needed
  3. Use module dependencies to understand high-level structure first

docs.title: Syntax Errors
docs.text:
Files with syntax errors are skipped: 

Syntax error in src/broken.py: invalid syntax

The analysis continues with remaining files.

docs.title: No Python Files Found
docs.text:
No Python files found!

Check that: - Path exists and is correct - Directory contains .py files - You have read permissions

docs.title: Import Resolution
docs.text:
D4-Diag resolves imports by: 1. Building a module map from analyzed files 2. Matching import statements to project files 3. External imports (e.g., numpy) are ignored

Only project-local imports create edges in the module dependency diagram.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Diagram Types - Understand each diagram
  • Viewing Diagrams - Interactive viewer features
  • CLI Reference - Complete command reference

docs.title: Diagram Types
docs.text: D4-Diag generates three complementary diagrams to help you understand your codebase.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
File: architecture.mmd

Shows your project's file structure with each file as a subgraph containing its classes and functions.

docs.title: What It Shows
docs.text:
• Files as containers - Each Python file is a labeled box
  • Classes - Shown with method count (e.g., "User (3 methods)")
  • Functions - Top-level functions in each file
  • Import relationships - Arrows between files that import each other

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Understanding project structure
  • Seeing which files contain which components
  • Identifying cross-file dependencies
  • Onboarding new developers

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
graph LR
    subgraph models.py
        User["User (2 methods)"]
        Admin["Admin (1 methods)"]
    end

    subgraph main.py
        create_user["create_user"]
        main["main"]
    end

    main.py --> models.py

docs.title: Class Diagram
docs.text:
File: class_diagram.mmd

A UML-style class diagram showing all classes with their methods and inheritance.

docs.title: What It Shows
docs.text:
• Classes - All class definitions
  • Methods - All methods in each class (prefixed with +)
  • Inheritance - Arrows from base class to derived class

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Understanding class hierarchies
  • Seeing all methods at a glance
  • Identifying inheritance patterns
  • Designing new classes

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
classDiagram
    class User {
        +__init__()
        +greet()
    }

    class Admin {
        +__init__()
    }

    User <|-- Admin

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Only shows classes (not standalone functions)
  • Shows method names but not signatures
  • Doesn't show attributes/properties
  • External base classes may not show inheritance

docs.title: Module Dependencies
docs.text:
File: module_deps.mmd

A clean dependency graph showing which modules import from which.

docs.title: What It Shows
docs.text:
• Modules - Each Python file as a node
  • Import relationships - Arrows from importer to imported
  • Only project files - External imports (e.g., numpy) are excluded

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Understanding module coupling
  • Identifying circular dependencies
  • Planning refactoring
  • Seeing high-level architecture

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
graph LR
    main --> models
    main --> utils
    models --> utils

docs.title: Import Resolution
docs.text:
D4-Diag resolves imports by: 1. Building a module map: src/models.py → models 2. Matching import statements to project files 3. Creating edges for local imports only

Both import models and from models import User create the same edge.

docs.title: Choosing the Right Diagram
docs.text: Goal Use This Diagram See project structure Architecture Understand class hierarchy Class Diagram Find circular dependencies Module Dependencies Onboard new developer Architecture → Module Deps Plan refactoring Module Dependencies Design new feature Class Diagram → Architecture

docs.title: Diagram Sizes
docs.text:
For a typical project:

  • Architecture: Largest (shows everything)
  • Class Diagram: Medium (classes only)
  • Module Dependencies: Smallest (files only)

docs.title: Mermaid Syntax
docs.text: All diagrams use Mermaid syntax and can be: - Viewed in the D4-Diag viewer - Embedded in GitHub README - Rendered in VS Code with Mermaid extension - Converted to images with Mermaid CLI

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Viewing Diagrams - Interactive viewer features
  • Examples - Real-world diagram examples

docs.title: Programmatic Usage
docs.text: D4-Diag can be used as a Python library for programmatic diagram generation.

docs.title: New API (v0.2.0+)
docs.text: The enhanced API returns diagrams as a dictionary by default, with optional file saving.

docs.title: Basic Usage - Return Dictionary
docs.text:
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

# Setup
project_root = "/path/to/project"
files = find_python_files(project_root)

# Analyze
analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root)
analyzer.build_module_map(files)

for file_path in files:
    analyzer.analyze_file(file_path)

# Generate diagrams - returns dictionary, doesn't save files
diagrams = analyzer.generate_all()

# Access diagram content
print(diagrams['architecture.mmd'])
print(diagrams['class_diagram.mmd'])
print(diagrams['module_deps.mmd'])

docs.title: Save to Default Location
docs.text:
# Save to {project_root}/docs/diagrams
diagrams = analyzer.generate_all(save_files=True)

docs.title: Save to Custom Location
docs.text:
# Save to custom directory
diagrams = analyzer.generate_all(
    save_files=True,
    output_dir="/custom/output/path"
)

docs.title: CodeMapAnalyzer
docs.text: Main class for code analysis and diagram generation.

docs.title: Constructor
docs.text:
analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root: str)

Parameters: - project_root (str): Absolute path to project root directory

docs.title: build_module_map(file_paths: List[str])
docs.text:
Build mapping from module names to file paths for import resolution.

analyzer.build_module_map(file_paths)

docs.title: analyze_file(file_path: str)
docs.text:
Analyze a single Python file and extract classes, functions, and imports.

analyzer.analyze_file("/path/to/file.py")

docs.title: generate_all(save_files: bool = False, output_dir: Optional[str] = None) -> Dict[str, str]
docs.text:
Generate all three diagram types and return as dictionary.

diagrams = analyzer.generate_all(
    save_files=False,      # Set to True to save files
    output_dir=None        # Custom output directory (optional)
)

Parameters: - save_files (bool): If True, save diagrams to files (default: False) - output_dir (str, optional): Directory to save files. If None and save_files=True, defaults to {project_root}/docs/diagrams

Returns: - Dict[str, str]: Dictionary mapping filenames to mmd content - 'architecture.mmd': Architecture overview diagram - 'class_diagram.mmd': UML class diagram - 'module_deps.mmd': Module dependency graph

docs.title: Individual Diagram Methods
docs.text:
Generate specific diagrams:

# Generate individual diagrams
arch_content = analyzer.generate_architecture()
class_content = analyzer.generate_class_diagram()
deps_content = analyzer.generate_module_deps()

# Or save to file
analyzer.generate_architecture(output_file="/path/to/arch.mmd")

docs.title: print_summary()
docs.text:
Print analysis summary to stdout.

analyzer.print_summary()

docs.title: find_python_files(root_path: str) -> List[str]
docs.text:
Recursively find all Python files in a directory.

from d4_diag.main import find_python_files

files = find_python_files("/path/to/project")

docs.title: 1. Web Service Integration
docs.text:
from flask import Flask, jsonify
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/analyze', methods=['POST'])
def analyze_project():
    project_path = request.json['path']

    files = find_python_files(project_path)
    analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_path)
    analyzer.build_module_map(files)

    for fp in files:
        analyzer.analyze_file(fp)

    # Return diagrams as JSON
    diagrams = analyzer.generate_all()
    return jsonify(diagrams)

docs.title: 2. CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text:
import os
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

def generate_docs_for_ci():
    """Generate diagrams in CI pipeline"""
    project_root = os.getenv('CI_PROJECT_DIR', '.')

    files = find_python_files(project_root)
    analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_root)
    analyzer.build_module_map(files)

    for fp in files:
        analyzer.analyze_file(fp)

    # Save to docs directory for deployment
    analyzer.generate_all(
        save_files=True,
        output_dir=os.path.join(project_root, 'docs', 'diagrams')
    )

    print("Diagrams generated for documentation site")

docs.title: 3. Custom Processing
docs.text:
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

def analyze_and_report(project_path):
    """Analyze project and generate custom report"""
    files = find_python_files(project_path)
    analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_path)
    analyzer.build_module_map(files)

    for fp in files:
        analyzer.analyze_file(fp)

    # Get diagrams
    diagrams = analyzer.generate_all()

    # Custom processing
    report = {
        'file_count': len(analyzer.files),
        'class_count': sum(len(f['classes']) for f in analyzer.files.values()),
        'function_count': sum(len(f['functions']) for f in analyzer.files.values()),
        'import_count': len(analyzer.import_edges),
        'diagrams': {
            name: {
                'size': len(content),
                'lines': content.count('\n') + 1,
                'nodes': content.count('['),
                'edges': content.count('-->')
            }
            for name, content in diagrams.items()
        }
    }

    return report

docs.title: 4. Batch Processing
docs.text:
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files
import os

def analyze_multiple_projects(projects):
    """Analyze multiple projects and save diagrams"""
    results = {}

    for project_name, project_path in projects.items():
        print(f"Analyzing {project_name}...")

        files = find_python_files(project_path)
        analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_path)
        analyzer.build_module_map(files)

        for fp in files:
            analyzer.analyze_file(fp)

        # Save each project's diagrams
        output_dir = f"analysis_results/{project_name}"
        diagrams = analyzer.generate_all(save_files=True, output_dir=output_dir)

        results[project_name] = {
            'files': len(files),
            'diagrams': list(diagrams.keys()),
            'output': output_dir
        }

    return results

# Usage
projects = {
    'project-a': '/path/to/project-a',
    'project-b': '/path/to/project-b',
    'project-c': '/path/to/project-c'
}

results = analyze_multiple_projects(projects)

docs.title: Before (v0.1.0)
docs.text:
# Old API - always saved files
analyzer.generate_all(output_dir)

docs.title: After (v0.2.0+)
docs.text:
# New API - return dictionary by default
diagrams = analyzer.generate_all()

# Or save files (backward compatible)
diagrams = analyzer.generate_all(save_files=True, output_dir=output_dir)

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
from d4_diag.main import CodeMapAnalyzer, find_python_files

try:
    files = find_python_files(project_path)

    if not files:
        print("No Python files found")
        return

    analyzer = CodeMapAnalyzer(project_path)
    analyzer.build_module_map(files)

    for fp in files:
        analyzer.analyze_file(fp)  # Errors are logged, not raised

    diagrams = analyzer.generate_all()

except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error during analysis: {e}")

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• CLI Reference - Command-line usage
  • API Reference - Complete API documentation
  • Examples - More usage examples

docs.title: Viewing Diagrams
docs.text: The D4-Diag viewer provides an interactive web interface for browsing generated diagrams.

docs.title: Starting the Viewer
docs.text: poetry run viewer <diagrams_directory>

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
# After analyzing a project
poetry run main /path/to/project

# View the diagrams
poetry run viewer /path/to/project/docs/diagrams

docs.title: Viewer Interface
docs.text:
The viewer opens in your default browser with:

  • Tab navigation - Switch between diagram types
  • Scrollable canvas - Pan around large diagrams
  • Full-size rendering - Diagrams render at actual size
  • Lazy loading - Diagrams render only when their tab is clicked

docs.title: Tab Switching
docs.text: Click any tab to view that diagram: - Architecture - Class Diagram - Module Dependencies

docs.title: Scrolling
docs.text: Large diagrams are scrollable: - Horizontal scroll - Pan left/right - Vertical scroll - Pan up/down - Mouse wheel - Scroll vertically

docs.title: Zoom
docs.text: Use browser zoom controls: - Ctrl + / Cmd + - Zoom in - Ctrl - / Cmd - - Zoom out - Ctrl 0 / Cmd 0 - Reset zoom

docs.title: Lazy Rendering
docs.text: Diagrams are rendered on-demand when you click their tab. This prevents: - Slow initial page load - Rendering hidden elements (which causes tiny boxes) - Memory issues with many large diagrams

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
If a diagram fails to render, you'll see: 

Diagram render error: <error message>

Common causes: - Invalid Mermaid syntax (report as bug) - Diagram too large (increase browser memory)

docs.title: Mermaid Version
docs.text: The viewer uses Mermaid v10.9.0 for compatibility.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Mermaid is configured with: 

{
  startOnLoad: false,      // Lazy rendering
  maxTextSize: 500000,     // Support large diagrams
  maxEdges: 5000,          // Support complex graphs
  useMaxWidth: false       // Full-size rendering
}

docs.title: Browser Compatibility
docs.text: Tested on: - Chrome/Edge (recommended) - Firefox - Safari

docs.title: Large Diagrams
docs.text: For projects with many files: 1. Use browser zoom to get an overview 2. Scroll to focus on specific areas 3. Consider analyzing subdirectories separately

docs.title: Saving Diagrams
docs.text:
To save a diagram: 1. Right-click the diagram 2. Select "Save image as..." 3. Save as PNG or SVG

Alternatively, the .mmd files can be: - Opened in any Mermaid editor - Embedded in Markdown documentation - Converted to images with Mermaid CLI

docs.title: Sharing
docs.text: Share diagrams by: 1. Committing .mmd files to your repo 2. Using GitHub's Mermaid rendering in README 3. Hosting the viewer HTML (it's self-contained)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Diagram Types - Understand what each diagram shows
  • Examples - See real-world usage
